sourceMovie.title: Q & A
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Q & A (1990) stuck because it commits to its action premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Sidney Lumet's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The action and thriller blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Blood Work
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Q & A clicked for you, Blood Work (2002) is a strong next stop: both trade in action and thriller, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Q & A clicked for you, The Departed (2006) is a strong next stop: both trade in thriller and crime, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Q & A?
faq.answer: Try Blood Work, The Departed, American Psycho—each shares DNA with Q & A while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Q & A?
faq.answer: Q & A (1990) is best described as Action, Thriller, Crime. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Q & A worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 5.8/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this action lane.
